sources.notes: Count intervals of a permutation that contain a 21435 pattern as a subsequence.
Official tutorial reduces to minimal r for each l, enumerates only Left-Right-Min minimal 435 subsequences, and answers the remaining 2D queries with segment trees.

annotation.summary: Count subarrays of a permutation that contain a subsequence with relative order pattern 21435.
annotation.constraints: Sum of n is 1e6, requiring O(n log n) processing per test case.
annotation.core_idea: For each left boundary l, it is enough to find the minimum right boundary containing a 21435 pattern. Fixing the 21 part determines the smaller element; then one needs a 435 part with value_3 greater than value_2 and index_4 after index_1. Among 435 subsequences, only Left-Right-Min minimal ones matter. Scanning candidate '3' values from high to low shows that each i has at most one relevant '5': its nearest greater element on the right, and then the '4' is forced. These O(n) candidates reduce the answer to 2D dominance/range maximum queries handled by segment trees.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) per test case.

solution_variants.name: minimal-435-scanline
solution_variants.summary: Generate O(n) minimal 435 candidates with monotonic nearest-greater/nearest-smaller links, then use segment trees to compute the earliest right endpoint per left boundary.

tags.evidence: The reduced problem is answered by range maximum / 2D dominance queries over candidate pattern endpoints.
The editorial scans by the value of the '3' element from large to small to maintain relevant 435 candidates.
The solution exploits nearest greater/smaller structure and relative-order pattern constraints in a permutation.
Only minimal 435 candidates are kept because any dominated candidate can never improve the earliest right endpoint.
